From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Fastest rail-based transport systems

"High-speed train" redirects here. For the "High Speed Train" in the United Kingdom, see [InterCity 125](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/InterCity_125 "InterCity 125").

For fast railway services with speeds less than 200 km/h (125 mph), see [Higher-speed rail](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Higher-speed_rail "Higher-speed rail").

High-speed rail

[![Image 4](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/N700a-Mt.Fuji.jpg/330px-N700a-Mt.Fuji.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:N700a-Mt.Fuji.jpg)

The [Tokaido Shinkansen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tokaido_Shinkansen "Tokaido Shinkansen") high-speed line in Japan, with [Mount Fuji](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mount_Fuji "Mount Fuji") in the background. The Tokaido Shinkansen, which connects the cities of [Tokyo](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Tokyo "Tokyo") and [Osaka](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Osaka "Osaka"), was the world's first high-speed rail line.

[![Image 5](https://upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/CR400BF-G-5168%40IFP_%2820210128114403%29.jpg/330px-CR400BF-G-5168%40IFP_%2820210128114403%29.jpg)](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/File:CR400BF-G-5168@IFP_(20210128114403).jpg)

High-speed trains operated by [China Railway](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/China_Railway "China Railway") at [Beijing Chaoyang railway station](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Beijing_Chaoyang_railway_station "Beijing Chaoyang railway station"); China has the most extensive high-speed rail network in the world.

**High-speed rail** (**HSR**) is a type of [rail transport](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rail_transport "Rail transport") network utilising [trains](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Train "Train") that run significantly faster than those of traditional rail, using an integrated system of specialised [rolling stock](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Rolling_stock "Rolling stock") and dedicated [tracks](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Railway_track "Railway track"). While there is no single definition or standard that applies worldwide, lines built to handle speeds of at least 250 km/h (155 mph) or upgraded lines of at least 200 km/h (125 mph) are generally considered to be high-speed.[[1]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-speed_rail#cite_note-1)[[2]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-speed_rail#cite_note-2)[[3]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-speed_rail#cite_note-3)

The first high-speed rail system, the [Tōkaidō Shinkansen](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/T%C5%8Dkaid%C5%8D_Shinkansen "Tōkaidō Shinkansen"), began operations in [Honshu](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Honshu "Honshu"), Japan, in 1964. Due to the streamlined [spitzer](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Spitzer_(bullet) "Spitzer (bullet)")-shaped [nose cone](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nose_cone "Nose cone") of the [trains](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/0_Series_Shinkansen "0 Series Shinkansen"), the system also became known by its English nickname [bullet train](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Shinkansen "Shinkansen"). Japan's example was followed by several European countries, initially in [Italy](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-speed_rail_in_Italy "High-speed rail in Italy") with the [Direttissima](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Florence%E2%80%93Rome_high-speed_railway "Florence–Rome high-speed railway") line, followed shortly thereafter by [France](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-speed_rail_in_France "High-speed rail in France"), [Germany](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-speed_rail_in_Germany "High-speed rail in Germany"), and [Spain](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-speed_rail_in_Spain "High-speed rail in Spain"). Today, much of Europe has an [extensive network](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-speed_rail_in_Europe "High-speed rail in Europe") with numerous international connections. Construction since the 21st century has led to China taking a leading role in high-speed rail. As of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=High-speed_rail&action=edit), [China's HSR network](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/High-speed_rail_in_China "High-speed rail in

**high-speed rail (HSR)**, passenger [train](https://www.britannica.com/technology/locomotive-vehicle) that generally travels at least 200 km (124 miles) per hour and can cruise up to 355 km (221 miles) per hour, though some have reached higher speeds. More than 20 countries, largely in [Asia](https://www.britannica.com/place/Asia) and [Europe](https://www.britannica.com/place/Europe), have high-speed rail networks. [Transportation](https://www.britannica.com/technology/transportation-technology) researchers have found that traveling via high-speed rail in Asia and Europe is a competitive [alternative](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/alternative) to [flying](https://www.britannica.com/technology/airplane) for trips up to about 1,000 km (620 miles).

History
-------

The first high-speed rail was [Japan’s](https://www.britannica.com/place/Japan) 515-km (320-mile) [Shinkansen](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Shinkansen) line connecting [Tokyo](https://www.britannica.com/place/Tokyo) and [Ōsaka](https://www.britannica.com/place/Osaka-Japan), inaugurated in advance of the 1964 Summer Olympics. Its inauguration was greeted by widespread international acclaim, and the Shinkansen was quickly dubbed the “bullet train” for the great speed the trains obtained and for the aerodynamic bullet shape of their noses. Many innovations, such as the use of prestressed concrete ties and 1.6-km- (1-mile-) long welded sections of track, were introduced in the line’s construction.

Japan’s impressive high-speed rail helped inspire the spread of the [technology](https://www.britannica.com/technology/technology) to other parts of the world. Europe’s first high-speed line opened in [Italy](https://www.britannica.com/place/Italy) in 1977, between [Rome](https://www.britannica.com/place/Rome) and [Florence](https://www.britannica.com/place/Florence). It was followed by [France](https://www.britannica.com/place/France) in 1981, with service between [Paris](https://www.britannica.com/place/Paris) and [Lyon](https://www.britannica.com/place/Lyon-France). In Canada, American-built [turbo trains](https://www.britannica.com/technology/turbo-train) operated in the 1970s between [Montreal](https://www.britannica.com/place/Montreal) and [Toronto](https://www.britannica.com/place/Toronto) but were eventually abandoned. Korean high-speed rail was first established in 2004 on a section of the[Seoul](https://www.britannica.com/place/Seoul)-[Pusan](https://www.britannica.com/place/Busan) line; the entire line was completed in 2010. In 2007 the Channel Tunnel Rail Link (CTRL), also called High Speed 1, was opened to connect the [Channel Tunnel](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Channel-Tunnel) with London,facilitating even greater movement of international passenger traffic between mainland Europe and the United Kingdom. In 2018 the Al Boraq line, connecting [Casablanca](https://www.britannica.com/place/Casablanca-Morocco) and [Tangier](https://www.britannica.com/place/Tangier-Morocco) in Morocco, gave Africa its first high-speed [railway](https://www.britannica.com/technology/railroad). There are no high-speed rail lines operating in Latin America.

[![Image 21: high-speed train](https://cdn.britannica.com/28/233928-050-CC617C2B/high-speed-railway-commuter-train.jpg) More From Britannica railroad: High-speed passenger lines](https://www.britannica.com/technology/railroad/High-speed-passenger-lines#ref919230)
[China](https://www.britannica.com/place/China), which had no high-speed rail at the start of the 21st century, aggressively built up these railways and now leads the world in this mode of [transportation](https://www.britannica.com/dictionary/transportation). With a network of lines covering roughly 38,000 km (24,000 miles), high-speed rail connects all of the country’s major megacity clusters. China plans to expand its high-speed railway network to nearly 70,000 km (43,000 miles) by 2035. China’s ruling [Communist Party](https://www.britannica.com/topic/Chinese-Communist-Party) has showcased the successful development of this modern transportation as evidence of the country’s economic power, technology, and improved [standard of living](https://www.britannica.com/money/standard-of-living).

The [United States](https://www.britannica.com/place/United-States), which has focused more resources on highways than trains, has struggled to develop high-speed rail on a widespread basis, despite taking steps dating back to the 1960s to do so. In 1965 Congress passed the High-Speed Ground Transportation Act, aimed at developing and demonstrating high-speed ground technologies. The [initiative](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/initiative) largely fell flat, however. [Turbo trains](https://www.britannica.com/technology/turbo-train) operated in the 1970s between[Boston](https://www.britannica.com/place/Boston)and[New York City](https://www.britannica.com/place/New-York-City)but were then abandoned. French-designed models were still operating in[New York](https://www.britannica.com/place/New-York-state)state in the mid-1980s but were also discontinued. In the first quarter of the 21st century, America had only a single high-speed rail line, the Acela in the Northeast, between [Boston](https://www.britannica.com/place/Boston) and [Washington, D.C.](https://www.britannica.com/place/Washington-DC) The Acela travels at speeds up to 241 km (150 miles) per hour in some sections, but the corridor has structural challenges that keep trains from moving at that speed throughout the route. Those include curves, which slow down trains; some bridges and tunnels that are more than 100 years old; and having to share the tracks with other trains, including freight. Amtrak’s goal is to shorten the Acela’s trip between New York and Washington to less than two hours. Although voters in [California](https://www.britannica.com/place/California-state), the most populous state in the U.S., approved a 2008 bond issue for a bullet train between [San Francisco](https://www.britannica.com/place/San-Francisco-California) and [Los Angeles](https://www.britannica.com/place/Los-Angeles-California), by 2022 construction had begun on only one section and the estimated cost had swollen to $113 billion—leaving its fate uncertain.

High-speed rail proponents in the U.S. were encouraged by the 2021 [Infrastructure](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/Infrastructure) Investment and Jobs Act, a $1 trillion infrastructure law that provided $66 billion for rail. That money is aimed at modernizing [Amtrak](https://www.britannica.com/money/Amtrak) and speeding up train times, including replacing the vintage bridges and tunnels that slow down rail traffic. Alstom, a French train manufacturer, is [building](https://www.britannica.com/technology/building) the trains for Amtrak in Hornell, New York, modeled on the French high-speed passenger trains (_trains à grande vitesse_; TGV). They will be the fastest in the United States, capable of traveling nearly 300 km (186 miles) per hour, although the trains will not hit that speed initially due to the corridor’s built-in limitations.

Technology
----------

[![Image 25: Shinkansen, Fuji, Japan](https://cdn.britannica.com/96/129096-050-99F5852B/Shinkansen-Japan-bridge-Fuji-River-Shizuoka-prefecture.jpg?w=300)](https://cdn.britannica.com/96/129096-050-99F5852B/Shinkansen-Japan-bridge-Fuji-River-Shizuoka-prefecture.jpg)

[Shinkansen, Fuji, Japan](https://cdn.britannica.com/96/129096-050-99F5852B/Shinkansen-Japan-bridge-Fuji-River-Shizuoka-prefecture.jpg)Japan's Shinkansen (bullet train) on a bridge over the Fuji River in Fuji, Shizuoka prefecture, Japan; Mount Fuji is in the background.(more)

Most high-speed rail lines use steel wheels that travel over steel rails, like conventional trains. Older [turbo trains](https://www.britannica.com/technology/turbo-train), used in parts of [North America](https://www.britannica.com/place/North-America) in the 1970s and ’80s, were powered by a gas-turbine engine similar to that used in jet aircraft. These types of high-speed rails have largely been discontinued in favour of electric trains. Japan’s Shinkansen are electric multiple-unit trains that derive their power from an overhead wire system. Sometimes seating 1,000 passengers or more and operating at more than 320 km (200 miles) per hour, electric trains have elaborate safety features. Each car, for example, is equipped with specialized braking systems designed not to distort under emergency braking. Moreover, all movements of the trains are monitored and controlled by a central computerized facility, and the rails are monitored for possible obstructions.

[![Image 26: maglev train](https://cdn.britannica.com/04/235404-050-743C687A/Shanghai-maglev-train.jpg?w=300)](https://cdn.britannica.com/04/235404-050-743C687A/Shanghai-maglev-train.jpg)

[maglev train](https://cdn.britannica.com/04/235404-050-743C687A/Shanghai-maglev-train.jpg)A maglev train, Shanghai.(more)

[Maglev trains](https://www.britannica.com/technology/maglev-train), also known as magnetic levitation trains, are another technology used to power fast rail service. Maglev trains use electromagnetic force to levitate several inches over the track or guideway. By eliminating a key source of friction—that of the wheels on the rails—such trains can reach higher speeds than conventional trains, have longer lasting parts, and are very quiet and smooth to ride. One challenge in building out maglev trains is that they require new infrastructure that cannot be [integrated](https://www.merriam-webster.com/dictionary/integrated) with existing railroads. Besides the costs of construction, another factor to be considered in developing maglev rail systems is that they require the use of rare-earth elements ([scandium](https://www.britannica.com/science/scandium), [yttrium](https://www.britannica.com/science/yttrium), and 15 [lanthanides](https://www.britannica.com/science/lanthanide)), which may be quite expensive to recover and refine. [Magnets](https://www.britannica.com/science/magnet) made from rare-earth elements, however, produce a stronger [magnetic field](https://www.britannica.com/science/magnetic-field) than[ferrite](https://www.britannica.com/science/ferrite-iron-oxide-compound)(iron compounds) or [alnico](https://www.britannica.com/technology/alnico) ([alloys](https://www.britannica.com/technology/alloy) of iron,aluminum,nickel,cobalt, and copper) magnets to lift and guide the train cars over a guideway.

In 2021 China, which has run a maglev train on a short route between an [airport](https://www.britannica.com/technology/airport) in [Shanghai](https://www.britannica.com/place/Shanghai) and a station in the city centre, announced that the speed of its maglev train could hit 600 km (373 miles) per hour. That would make it the fastest ground vehicle in the world. There are also short maglev train routes in Japan and [South Korea](https://www.britannica.com/place/South-Korea).

# Higher-speed rail

![The Amtrak Southwest Chief](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ae/Southwest_Chief_at_Los_Cerrillos%2C_February_2020.jpg/250px-Southwest_Chief_at_Los_Cerrillos%2C_February_2020.jpg)

![China Railway CR200J](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/26/FXD1-J0010_at_Guangqumen_%2820190203093259%29.jpg/250px-FXD1-J0010_at_Guangqumen_%2820190203093259%29.jpg)
![VIA Rail's Corridor service east of Toronto](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/85/VIA_2311.jpg/250px-VIA_2311.jpg)

**Higher-speed rail** (**HrSR**)[[a]](#cite_note-4) is used to describe [inter-city passenger rail](/wiki/Inter-city_rail "Inter-city rail") services that have top speeds of more than conventional rail but are not high enough to be called [high-speed rail](/wiki/High-speed_rail "High-speed rail") services.[[4]](#cite_note-5)[[5]](#cite_note-Borges-6) The term is also used by planners to identify the incremental rail improvements to increase train speeds and reduce travel time as alternatives to larger efforts to create or expand the high-speed rail networks.[[6]](#cite_note-7)

Though the definition of higher-speed rail varies from country to country, most countries refer to rail services operating at speeds up to 200 km/h (125 mph).[[7]](#cite_note-India-8)

The concept is usually viewed as stemming from efforts to upgrade a legacy railway line to high speed railway standards (speeds in excess of 250 km/h or 155 mph), but usually falling short on the intended speeds. The faster speeds are achieved through various means including new rolling stock such as tilting trains, upgrades to tracks including shallower curves, electrification, in-cab signalling, and less frequent halts/stops.[[8]](#cite_note-9)

## Definitions by country

As with the [definitions of high-speed rail](/wiki/High-speed_rail#Definitions "High-speed rail"), the definition varies by country. The term has been used by government agencies,[[9]](#cite_note-10) government officials,[[10]](#cite_note-11) transportation planners,[[11]](#cite_note-nctcograil-12) academia,[[12]](#cite_note-13) the rail industry,[[13]](#cite_note-14) and the media,[[14]](#cite_note-15) but sometimes with overlaps in the speed definitions. Some countries with an established definition of higher-speed rail include:

| Agency / Council | Top speeds (mph) | Ref |
| --- | --- | --- |
| [California Department of Transportation](/wiki/California_Department_of_Transportation "California Department of Transportation") | Up to 125 | [[22]](#cite_note-23) |
| [Minnesota Department of Transportation](/wiki/Minnesota_Department_of_Transportation "Minnesota Department of Transportation") | Greater than 90 but less than 125 | [[23]](#cite_note-24) |
| [North Central Texas Council of Governments](/wiki/North_Central_Texas_Council_of_Governments "North Central Texas Council of Governments") | 80–150 | [[11]](#cite_note-nctcograil-12) |
| [Oklahoma Department of Transportation](/wiki/Oklahoma_Department_of_Transportation "Oklahoma Department of Transportation") | 110–125 | [[24]](#cite_note-txok-25) |
| [Texas Department of Transportation](/wiki/Texas_Department_of_Transportation "Texas Department of Transportation") | 110–125 | [[24]](#cite_note-txok-25) |
| [Virginia Department of Rail and Public Transportation](/wiki/Virginia_Department_of_Rail_and_Public_Transportation "Virginia Department of Rail and Public Transportation") | Up to 110 | [[19]](#cite_note-drpt-20) |

## Speed limits

In Canada, the assumption about grade crossing is that operating higher-speed rail services between 160 and 200 km/h (99 and 124 mph) would require "improved levels of protection in acceptable areas".[[25]](#cite_note-qohsrps-26)

In the United States, railroad tracks are largely used for freight with [at-grade crossings](/wiki/At-grade_intersection "At-grade intersection"). Passenger trains in many corridors run on shared tracks with [freight trains](/wiki/Freight_rail_transport "Freight rail transport"). Most trains are limited to top speeds of 79 mph (127 km/h) unless they are equipped with an automatic [cab signal](/wiki/Cab_signalling "Cab signalling"), [automatic train stop](/wiki/Automatic_train_stop "Automatic train stop"), [automatic train control](/wiki/Automatic_train_control "Automatic train control") or [positive train control](/wiki/Positive_train_control "Positive train control") system approved by the [Federal Railroad Administration](/wiki/Federal_Railroad_Administration "Federal Railroad Administration") (FRA).[[26]](#cite_note-27) In developing higher-speed rail services, one of those safety systems must be used.

Additionally, the FRA establishes [classification of track quality](/wiki/Rail_speed_limits_in_the_United_States#Track_classes "Rail speed limits in the United States") which regulates the speed limits of trains with Class 5, Class 6, Class 7 and Class 8 for top speeds of 90 mph (145 km/h),[[27]](#cite_note-28) 110 mph (175 km/h), 125 mph (200 km/h) and 160 mph (255 km/h), respectively.[[28]](#cite_note-29) The FRA also regulates passenger train design and safety standards to ensure trains that operate at speeds of 80 mph (130 km/h) up to 125 mph (200 km/h) comply with its *Tier I* standard and trains that operate at speeds up to 150 mph (240 km/h) comply with its *Tier II* standard.[[29]](#cite_note-30)

Another limitation is the safety of [grade crossings](/wiki/Level_crossing "Level crossing") (also known as [level crossings](/wiki/Level_crossing "Level crossing"), [flat level crossings](/wiki/Grade_crossing "Grade crossing"), non-[grade-separated](/wiki/Grade_separation "Grade separation") crossings) which limits how fast trains can go. FRA regulations set speed limits for tracks with grade crossings as follows:[[30]](#cite_note-fracrossings-31) Level crossings are generally the most dangerous part of the railway network with a large number of fatal incidents occurring at a grade crossing.

In Europe, the limit is often 160 km/h (100 mph) over grade crossings.[[31]](#cite_note-32) In [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden") there is a special rule permitting 200 km/h (125 mph) if there are barriers and automatic detection of road vehicles standing on the track.[[32]](#cite_note-33) In Russia 250 km/h (155 mph) is permitted over grade crossings.[[33]](#cite_note-34) The United Kingdom has railway lines of 200 km/h (125 mph) which still use grade crossings.

With the above limitations, many regional transportation planners focus on rail improvements to have the top speeds up to 110 mph (175 km/h) when proposing a new higher-speed rail service.[[21]](#cite_note-crs-22)

## Similar categories

In countries where there had been rail improvement projects in the later part of the 20th century and into the 2000s, there are inter-city rail services with comparable speed ranges of higher-speed rail, but they are not specifically called "higher-speed rail". Below are some examples of such services that are still in operation.

## Rail improvement strategies

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/02/Amt_9634-CDU-closeup.jpg/250px-Amt_9634-CDU-closeup.jpg)

There are many types of trains that can support higher-speed rail operation. Usually, the rail infrastructure needs to be upgraded prior to such operation. However, the requirements to the infrastructure (signalling systems, curve radii, etc.) greatly increase with higher speeds, so an upgrade to a higher-speed standard is often simpler and less expensive than building new high-speed lines. But an upgrade to existing track currently in use, with busy traffic in some segments, introduces challenges associated with the construction work that could potentially disrupt the train services. The followings are some strategies used by regional transportation planners and rail track owners for their rail improvement projects in order to start the higher-speed rail services.

### Signal upgrades

In [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia) "Victoria (Australia)"), Australia, the increased top speeds from 130 to 160 km/h (80 to 100 mph) in the [Regional Fast Rail project](/wiki/Regional_Fast_Rail_project "Regional Fast Rail project") required a change to the signalling system to account for increased braking distance. Prior to the project, the system comprised a mixture of equipment from pre-[WWI](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I") mechanical signalling to the remote control systems of the 1980s. In some cases, operators needed to telephone the local operators to manually control the signal boxes. With the new speeds, the signalling needed to be computerized. The project employed the [Solid State Interlocking](/wiki/Solid_State_Interlocking "Solid State Interlocking") with the newly laid [fiber-optic](/wiki/Fiber-optic "Fiber-optic") communication between the components to use three computer systems to control the signals. When the output of one computer differs from the other two, the system will fail that computer and continue the signal operations as long as the outputs from the other two computers are consistent. The project deployed the [Train Protection & Warning System](/wiki/Train_Protection_%26_Warning_System "Train Protection & Warning System") which allows the system to automatically applies the brakes at a sufficient distance to stop the train if the driver does not control the speeds adequately. The project also incorporated Train Control and Monitoring System to allow real-time monitoring of the position of trains.[[49]](#cite_note-rfr-50)

In the United States, the first step to increase top speeds from 79 mph (127 km/h) is to install a new signal system that incorporates FRA-approved positive train control (PTC) system that is compatible with higher-speed rail operation.[[50]](#cite_note-51) They are both [transponder](/wiki/Transponder "Transponder")-based and [GPS](/wiki/Global_Positioning_System "Global Positioning System")-based PTC systems currently in use in the United States. By a mandate, a significant portion of the railroads in the United States will be covered by PTC by the end of 2015.[[51]](#cite_note-52)

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/62/Concrete_sleeper_1638.JPG/250px-Concrete_sleeper_1638.JPG)

### Track improvements

To support trains that run regularly at higher speeds, the rails need to be reliable. Most freight tracks have [wooden ties](/wiki/Railroad_tie#Wooden "Railroad tie") which cause rails to become slightly misaligned over time due to wood rot, splitting and [spike](/wiki/Rail_fastening_system "Rail fastening system")-pull (where the spike is gradually loosened from the tie).[[52]](#cite_note-53) The [concrete ties](/wiki/Concrete_sleeper "Concrete sleeper") used to replace them are intended to make the track more stable, particularly with changes in temperature.[[53]](#cite_note-54) Rail joints are also an issue, since most conventional rail lines use bolts and fishplates to join two sections of the rail together. This causes the joint to become slightly misaligned over time due to loosening bolts. To make for a smoother ride at higher speeds, the lengths of rail may be welded together to form [continuous welded rail](/wiki/Track_(rail_transport)#Continuous_welded_rail "Track (rail transport)") (CWR). However, the continuous welded rails are vulnerable to stress due to changes in temperature.[[54]](#cite_note-55)

In Australia, the track condition before the Regional Fast Rail project could only support trains up to speeds of 130 km/h (80 mph). The tracks are with mixture of wooden and concrete ties. The [rail weight](/wiki/Rail_weight "Rail weight") varies but with majority being 47 kg/m (95 lb/yd). The track upgrade in the project included changing to use concrete ties and to use new standard of rail weight at 60 kg/m (121 lb/yd) in order to support the new top speeds of 160 km/h (100 mph).[[55]](#cite_note-56)

There may be restriction in maximum operating speeds due to [track geometry](/wiki/Track_geometry "Track geometry") of existing line, especially on curves. Straightening the route, where possible, will reduce the travel time by increasing the allowable speeds and by reducing the length of track. When straight routes are not possible, reducing the number of curves and lowering the [degree of curvature](/wiki/Degree_of_curvature "Degree of curvature") would result in higher achievable speeds on those curves. An example is the elimination of three consecutive [reverse curves](/wiki/Track_geometry#Curvature "Track geometry") in favor of one larger curve. Raising [superelevation](/wiki/Cant_(road/rail) "Cant (road/rail)") may be considered for sharp curves which significantly limit speed. The higher speeds on those modified curves, together with the higher superelevation, will require track modification to have [transition spirals](/wiki/Track_transition_curve "Track transition curve") to and from those curves to be longer.[[56]](#cite_note-57)

Old [turnouts](/wiki/Railroad_switch "Railroad switch") may need replacement to allow trains to run through the turnouts at higher speeds. In the United States, some old turnouts have speed limit of 20 mph (30 km/h). Even with newer turnouts (rated #20), the diverging speed limit is still at 45 mph (70 km/h) which would significantly slow down the higher-speed train passing through those sections. High-speed turnouts (rated #32.7) are capable of handling maximum diverging speeds of 80 mph (130 km/h).[[57]](#cite_note-58)[[58]](#cite_note-59)[[59]](#cite_note-60)

In order to minimize the downtime to upgrade tracks, a [track renewal train](/wiki/Track_renewal_train "Track renewal train") (TRT) can automate much of the process, replacing rails, ties, and ballast at the rate of 2 miles per day. In the United States, a TRT is used by [Union Pacific Railroad](/wiki/Union_Pacific_Railroad "Union Pacific Railroad") on the track shared with future higher-speed rail service in Illinois area.[[60]](#cite_note-61)[[61]](#cite_note-62)

For electrified track, the old [catenary](/wiki/Overhead_line "Overhead line") may need to be replaced. The fixed-tension catenary which is acceptable for low speeds may not be suitable for regular higher-speed rail services, where a [constant tension](/wiki/Catenary_(rail)#Tensioning "Catenary (rail)") is automatically maintained when temperature changes cause the length of the wire to expand or contract.[[62]](#cite_note-63)

### Crossing improvements

With trains running at higher speeds throughout the route, safety at all at-grade crossings needs to be considered.

In Australia, the levels of upgrade of the crossing in the rail improvements project were based on the risk analysis. The improvements included flashing light protection, [automatic full barriers](/wiki/Automatic_full_barriers "Automatic full barriers") protection, and pedestrian gates crossings. The project also introduced the use of rubber panels at the crossings.[[49]](#cite_note-rfr-50)

In the United States, the FRA limits train speeds to 110 mph (175 km/h) without an "impenetrable barrier" at each crossing. Even with that top speed, the grade crossings must have adequate means to prevent collisions. Another option is [grade separation](/wiki/Grade_separation "Grade separation"), but it could be cost-prohibitive and the planners may opt for at-grade crossing improvements instead.[[30]](#cite_note-fracrossings-31)

The safety improvements at crossings can be done using combination of techniques. This includes passive devices such as upgraded signage and pavement markings. Another low-cost passive device is [median separators](/wiki/Raised_pavement_marker#Delineator "Raised pavement marker") which are installed along the center line of roadways, extending approximately 70 to 100 feet from the crossing, to discourage drivers from running around the crossing gates. More active devices include the [four-quadrant gate](/wiki/Four-quadrant_gate "Four-quadrant gate"), which blocks both sides of each traffic lane. Longer gate arms can cover 3/4 of the roadway. Video cameras can also be installed to catch the violators. A signal monitoring system can also be installed to alert the crews when the crossing equipment has malfunctioned.[[63]](#cite_note-64)

In Norway, grade crossing speed are not permitted to exceed 160 km/h (100 mph).[[64]](#cite_note-65)

### Rerouting and passing sidings

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4c/NS_Entering_the_NE_Corridor.jpg/250px-NS_Entering_the_NE_Corridor.jpg)

In areas where there is frequent interference between freight and passenger trains due to congestion which causes the passenger trains to slow down, more extensive improvements may be needed. Certain segments of the line in congested areas may need to be rerouted. New track may need to be laid to avoid many curves which slow down the trains. In stretches of heavy freight train traffic, adding [passing sidings](/wiki/Passing_loop "Passing loop") along the segment should be considered. Sometimes certain stations may need to be bypassed.[[65]](#cite_note-66)

### Electrification

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/08/Amtrak_Keystone_Corridor_Rosemont_Curve.jpg/250px-Amtrak_Keystone_Corridor_Rosemont_Curve.jpg)

Another consideration is [electrification](/wiki/Railway_electrification_system "Railway electrification system"). Electrifying a railway line entails a major upgrade to the rail infrastructure and equipment. On the infrastructure side, it requires [catenary lines](/wiki/Overhead_line "Overhead line") to be built above the tracks. New transmission lines are needed to carry power from the power plants. [Substations](/wiki/Electrical_substation "Electrical substation") are required for each of the 40-mile (64 km) lengths to reduce severe voltage losses. There is also a need to consider the required amount of power supply and new power plants may be required. For locomotives, new electric locomotives are needed or existing [diesel-electric locomotives](/wiki/Diesel-electric_locomotive "Diesel-electric locomotive") can be retrofitted into all-electric locomotives, but it is a complicated task.[[66]](#cite_note-67) These factors cause electrification to have high initial investment costs. The advantages of all-electric locomotives are that they provide quieter, cleaner and more reliable operations than the diesel-electric counterpart. The fuel consumption, locomotive maintenance costs and track wear of all all-electric locomotives are also lower.[[67]](#cite_note-68) Furthermore, electric traction makes the operator more independent of oil price fluctuations and imports, as electricity can be generated from domestic resources or renewable energy. This was a major consideration in the electrification of the [German Democratic Republic network](/wiki/Deutsche_Reichsbahn_(East_Germany)#Electrification "Deutsche Reichsbahn (East Germany)"), as [lignite](/wiki/Lignite "Lignite") (and therefore electricity) was cheap and plentiful domestically whereas oil had to be imported at world market prices.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

An alternative to catenary lines is to use a [third rail](/wiki/Third_rail "Third rail") system which has a semi-continuous rigid conductor placed alongside or between the rails of a railway track. However the operating speeds of this type of systems cannot be greater than 100 mph (160 km/h) due to its limitation of the power supply gaps at turnouts and grade crossings. Therefore, the third rail system is not generally used for higher-speed rail.[[68]](#cite_note-69)

One example in the United States that does involve electrification is the Keystone Improvement Project to provide higher-speed rail service along the [Harrisburg](/wiki/Harrisburg,_Pennsylvania "Harrisburg, Pennsylvania")-[Pittsburgh](/wiki/Pittsburgh "Pittsburgh") segment of the [Keystone Corridor](/wiki/Keystone_Corridor "Keystone Corridor") in [Pennsylvania](/wiki/Pennsylvania "Pennsylvania"). The plan includes additional track, a new signal system and electrification. If completed as planned, this would allow [Amtrak](/wiki/Amtrak "Amtrak") to utilize electric power continuously on service from [Philadelphia](/wiki/Philadelphia "Philadelphia") to Pittsburgh. The first segment ("Main Line") has already been using [electric locomotives](/wiki/Electric_locomotives "Electric locomotives") with a top speed of 110 mph (175 km/h).[[69]](#cite_note-70)

### Australia

![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fa/Countrylink_XPT_at_Sydney_Central_station.jpg/250px-Countrylink_XPT_at_Sydney_Central_station.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/54/QRTILTRAIN2.jpg/250px-QRTILTRAIN2.jpg)

In 1999, the concept of [Regional Fast Rail project](/wiki/Regional_Fast_Rail_project "Regional Fast Rail project") was initiated by the [Government of Victoria](/wiki/Government_of_Victoria "Government of Victoria") with a goal to provide express higher-speed rail services between four main regional centres of Victoria ([Geelong](/wiki/Geelong "Geelong"), [Ballarat](/wiki/Ballarat "Ballarat"), [Bendigo](/wiki/Bendigo "Bendigo") and the [Latrobe Valley](/wiki/Latrobe_Valley "Latrobe Valley")) and [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne "Melbourne"). The initiative included a key component to upgrade rail infrastructure to have top speeds up to 160 km/h (100 mph). The development phase of initiative was between 2000 and 2002. Finally, the services on four lines began between 2005 and 2006 with top speeds of 160 km/h using [VLocity](/wiki/V/Line_VLocity "V/Line VLocity") trains.[[73]](#cite_note-74) Additionally, [Queensland Rail](/wiki/Queensland_Rail "Queensland Rail")'s [Tilt Train](/wiki/Tilt_Train "Tilt Train"), the [Prospector](/wiki/The_Prospector_(train) "The Prospector (train)") and [NSW TrainLink's XPT](/wiki/New_South_Wales_XPT "New South Wales XPT") all have a top service speed of 160 km/h (99–100 mph).[[74]](#cite_note-75)[[75]](#cite_note-76)[[76]](#cite_note-77)

The New South Wales XPT (short for Express Passenger Train) is the main long-distance passenger train operated by NSW TrainLink on regional railway services in [New South Wales](/wiki/New_South_Wales "New South Wales"), Australia from [Sydney](/wiki/Central_railway_station,_Sydney "Central railway station, Sydney") to [Dubbo](/wiki/Dubbo_railway_station "Dubbo railway station"), [Grafton](/wiki/Grafton_railway_station,_New_South_Wales "Grafton railway station, New South Wales"), and [Casino](/wiki/Casino_railway_station "Casino railway station") as well as interstate destinations, [Brisbane](/wiki/Roma_Street_railway_station "Roma Street railway station") and [Melbourne](/wiki/Southern_Cross_railway_station "Southern Cross railway station"). The XPT is based on the [British Rail](/wiki/British_Rail "British Rail") designed [High Speed Train](/wiki/InterCity_125 "InterCity 125") and entered service in April 1982. It came to fruition in January 1978 when the [Public Transport Commission](/wiki/Public_Transport_Commission "Public Transport Commission") invited tenders for 25 high-speed [railcars](/wiki/Railcar "Railcar") similar to the [Prospector railcars](/wiki/WAGR_WCA/WCE_class "WAGR WCA/WCE class") delivered by [Comeng](/wiki/Commonwealth_Engineering "Commonwealth Engineering") to the [Western Australian Government Railways](/wiki/Western_Australian_Government_Railways "Western Australian Government Railways") in 1971. Comeng's proposal for a train based on the InterCity 125 was announced as the successful bidder in October 1976.

The Tilt Train is the name for two similar [tilting train](/wiki/Tilting_train "Tilting train") services, one [electric](/wiki/Electric_Tilt_Train "Electric Tilt Train") and the other [diesel](/wiki/Diesel_Tilt_Train "Diesel Tilt Train"), operated by [Queensland Rail](/wiki/Queensland_Rail "Queensland Rail") on the [North Coast line](/wiki/North_Coast_railway_line,_Queensland "North Coast railway line, Queensland") from [Brisbane](/wiki/Roma_Street_railway_station "Roma Street railway station") to [Rockhampton](/wiki/Rockhampton_railway_station "Rockhampton railway station") and [Cairns](/wiki/Cairns_railway_station "Cairns railway station"). In May 1999 the [Electric Tilt Train](/wiki/Electric_Tilt_Train "Electric Tilt Train") set an Australian train speed record of 210 km/h (130 mph) north of [Bundaberg](/wiki/Bundaberg "Bundaberg"), a record that still stands.[[77]](#cite_note-78)[[78]](#cite_note-79)

The [Transwa WDA/WDB/WDC class](/wiki/Transwa_WDA/WDB/WDC_class "Transwa WDA/WDB/WDC class") are a class of railcars built by [United Goninan](/wiki/UGL_Rail "UGL Rail"), [Broadmeadow](/wiki/Broadmeadow "Broadmeadow") for [Transwa](/wiki/Transwa "Transwa") in 2004–05 to replace the [WAGR WCA/WCE class](/wiki/WAGR_WCA/WCE_class "WAGR WCA/WCE class") railcars on the *[AvonLink](/wiki/AvonLink "AvonLink")* and *[Prospector](/wiki/The_Prospector_(train) "The Prospector (train)")* services in [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"). They are capable of high-speed operation.

### China

![Railway Lines in China that run CRH services](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/93/High_and_Higher_speed_rail_in_China.svg/500px-High_and_Higher_speed_rail_in_China.svg.png)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c6/CRH6F-A-0496%40FGP_%2820201009113705%29.jpg/250px-CRH6F-A-0496%40FGP_%2820201009113705%29.jpg)
![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/af/China_Railways_train_K9787_on_Nanjiang_Railway_line.jpg/250px-China_Railways_train_K9787_on_Nanjiang_Railway_line.jpg)

In China, higher-speed railways are railways that are not officially categorized as high-speed rail but allow [CRH EMUs](/wiki/China_Railway_High-speed "China Railway High-speed") run on it with speeds up to 200 km/h.[[79]](#cite_note-80) Typically these lines are classified as Grade I conventional railways and are used by both passenger and freight services.

Note that the majority of high-speed lines are also called "passenger-only"([Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 客运专线) lines. Inside mainland China this word invokes a sense of higher-speed rail but the wording usage is inconsistent.

#### Train identifiers

Identifiers starting with G indicates at least part of the train's route operates at a maximum 300 km/h or above (this is a characteristic of the line rather than the precise maximum speed of this exact train) and not running at deliberately reduced speed on any section. Other sections of the route may have lower speeds as low as 160 km/h.

Identifiers starting with C indicates short-distance travel using CRH trains, the maximum speed is irrelevant (ranging from 160 km/h Ürümqi-Korla service to 350 km/h Beijing-Tianjin (via intercity) service).

Identifiers starting with D indicates CRH services with maximum speed 265 km/h or less, including overnight sleepers on 310 km/h Beijing-Guangzhou line (running them 310 km/h overnight not only causes noises but also disturbs sleeping patterns of passengers. This is an example of deliberately reduced speeds).

Identifiers starting with S indicates metropolitan services using CRH rolling stock and have a different fare system to the national one. Their maximum speed is 160 km/h.

\* denotes some section of this line doesn't have 160 km/h CRH services.

enriched_points.expanded_content: High-Speed Rail (HSR) refers to trains operating on dedicated tracks at speeds exceeding 250 km/h, utilizing advanced technologies for efficiency and safety.

enriched_points.point_title: Importance of HSR in modern transportation
enriched_points.expanded_content: HSR plays a crucial role in reducing travel times, alleviating traffic congestion, and promoting sustainable transport options, especially in densely populated regions.
HSR offers numerous advantages, including lower carbon emissions, energy efficiency, reduced travel times, economic growth stimulation, and enhanced connectivity between urban centers.
